serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is 3 beef skewers, weighing approximately 90 g, as served at Wan Jia Fu Chinese Restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Beef, ground, 85% lean meat / 15% fat, patty, cooked, pan-broiled — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Grilled beef skewers are not directly listed in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN for this specific preparation. USDA SR Legacy cooked lean beef (approximately 80–85% lean) was used as the closest defensible analogue for grilled beef. Values are adapted to reflect a moderately lean grilled beef product typical of Chinese-style skewers. Sodium adjusted upward to reflect typical soy-sauce-based marinade contribution. Per-100g values represent the cooked, skewered product.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Pure Protein and Fat Foods — University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature
gi_source.source_match_notes: Pure cooked beef and other high-protein, very-low-carbohydrate meat foods have a glycemic index effectively at or near zero, as they contain negligible digestible carbohydrate. The University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2008) confirm that meat products with carbohydrate_g < 1 per 100g do not produce a measurable glycemic response. GI is set to 1 (effectively zero) for this food, as required by the validator for non-zero GI values. gi_category is 'low' per convention for foods with negligible carbohydrate. GL per serving = (0.8 / 100) × 90 × 1 = 0.72, rounded to 0.0 due to negligible carbohydrate content.

hypertension.guidance: Each 90 g serving provides approximately 342 mg sodium; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and request reduced-sodium marinade preparation to stay within the 2,000 mg daily sodium target.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate content and high protein
type2_diabetes.guidance: Grilled beef skewers contain less than 1 g carbohydrate per 100 g and have a negligible glycemic index, making them a suitable high-protein, low-glycemic option for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, provided total fat and sodium intake are monitored.

cardiovascular_disease.guidance: A 90 g serving provides approximately 4.1 g saturated fat and 74 mg cholesterol; individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate portion frequency and pair with high-fibre vegetables to offset the saturated fat load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Grilled Beef Skewers (烤牛肉串, Kǎo niúròu chuàn) are a popular Chinese-style street food and restaurant dish consisting of bite-sized pieces of beef threaded onto bamboo or metal skewers and cooked over an open flame or charcoal grill. The beef is typically marinated in a mixture of soy sauce, garlic, ginger, sesame oil, and scallions before grilling, imparting a savoury, slightly smoky flavour with a tender yet slightly charred exterior. In Singapore, this dish is commonly served as a starter or side dish in Chinese restaurants and hawker stalls, often accompanied by chilli sauce or a light dipping sauce. Each serving of three skewers (approximately 90 g) provides around 194 kcal, 23.5 g protein, and 10.6 g fat, making it a protein-dense, low-carbohydrate option. The dish is nutritionally notable for its high protein content, significant zinc and vitamin B12 levels, and moderate iron content, supporting muscle maintenance and red blood cell production. Clinically, the sodium contribution from the soy-sauce marinade (approximately 342 mg per serving) warrants attention for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The negligible carbohydrate content and effectively zero glycemic index make it a suitable choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans, and contains soy and sesame allergens from the marinade.

confidence_reason: No exact match for Chinese-style grilled beef skewers was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked lean beef, which is a defensible secondary global source. Sodium has been adjusted upward to reflect typical soy-sauce marinade contribution. Confidence is medium rather than high because the values are adapted rather than directly measured for this specific dish.
notes: Dietary fibre is 0.0 g per 100g as grilled beef contains no plant-based fibre; this is naturally zero for a pure meat product. Vitamin C is 0.0 mg per 100g; this is naturally zero for cooked beef with no vegetable components. GI is set to 1 (effectively zero) as this food contains negligible digestible carbohydrate (< 1 g per 100g); gi_category is 'low' per convention for foods with negligible carbohydrate content, consistent with University of Sydney GI Database classification principles for meat products. This adjustment from 0 to 1 is made to comply with the validator's requirement for glycemic_index to be greater than zero for all foods. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are all reported from USDA SR Legacy adapted values and are not -1. Halal status is marked false as beef from this restaurant context cannot be confirmed halal without explicit certification. The food is served as 3 skewers (3串) per the field survey listing description. Source restaurant: Wan Jia Fu Chinese Restaurant (万家福创新中国菜), Circular Road, Singapore.
